---
title: Lynn Ann Conway
date: 2026-04-30
draft: false
image: "lynn_conway.jpg"
---

:::: {layout="[ 60, -5, 40 ]"}

::: {#first-column}

Lynn Ann Conway was an American computer scientist and electrical engineer (1938–2024) whose technical career included 
early work at IBM, leadership at Xerox PARC and the University of Michigan. 
She is highly recognised for her work on generalized dynamic instruction handling, an important precursor to out-of-order execution.
With Carver Mead she coauthored "Introduction to VLSI Systems" and helped create the "Mead–Conway Very-Large-Scale-Integration (VLSI) methodology" that democratized chip design and shaped modern microelectronics. 

Conway’s faced immense workplace discrimination after revealing her intent to gender transition, which resulted to her being dismissed from IBM in 1968. 
This had effects on how and when she could claim credit for her work, leading to her having to live much of her professional life quietly under a different identity before coming out publicly in 1999 and documenting her story and resources for others. 
This experience shaped her later advocacy for transgender people and increased visibility for trans professionals in STEM. 

Her technical and social legacy persists even after her death in 2024: The Mead–Conway methods underpin electronic design automation and VLSI education worldwide. 
Her public advocacy, mentorship resources and the later formal apology from IBM have contributed to broader recognition of transgender scientists and institutional reckonings with past discrimination. 
Many engineers and educators trace current chip-design practices and more inclusive cultures in computing back to her influence. 

:::

::: {#second-column}

![Lynn Conway](lynn_conway.jpg){width=100%, fig-align=center}

[Photo credit](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Lynn_Conway_July_2006.jpg), available under public domain

:::

::::
